topics.description: Indiana General Assembly — composition, powers, and legislative processes

topics.description: Governor, lieutenant governor, and executive agencies

topics.description: Indiana court system, judiciary selection, and judicial powers

topics.name: Policy & Governance
topics.description: State policy areas, budget processes, taxation, education governance with civic context

questions.text: How many members serve in the Indiana House of Representatives?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, the Indiana House of Representatives has 100 members who serve 2-year terms. This makes it twice the size of the Indiana Senate, which has 50 members.

questions.text: How many members serve in the Indiana Senate?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, the Indiana Senate has 50 members who serve 4-year terms. This is half the size of the Indiana House of Representatives.

questions.text: What is the term length for an Indiana State Senator?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, Indiana State Senators serve 4-year terms. This is longer than the 2-year terms served by members of the Indiana House of Representatives.

questions.text: What is the term length for an Indiana State Representative?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, Indiana State Representatives serve 2-year terms. This is shorter than the 4-year terms served by Indiana State Senators.

questions.text: How many days does the Indiana General Assembly meet in odd-numbered years?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the General Assembly meets for 61 days in odd-numbered years and 30 days in even-numbered years. This part-time legislature model allows legislators to maintain other careers.

questions.text: How many days does the Indiana General Assembly meet in even-numbered years?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the General Assembly meets for 30 days in even-numbered years and 61 days in odd-numbered years. This shorter session in even-numbered years is designed for budget adjustments and urgent matters.

questions.text: What is the name of Indiana's state legislature?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, Indiana's state legislature is called the General Assembly. It consists of two chambers: the Senate and the House of Representatives.

questions.text: How many chambers make up the Indiana General Assembly?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the General Assembly is bicameral, meaning it has two chambers: the Senate and the House of Representatives. This structure mirrors the federal Congress.

questions.text: Which chamber of the Indiana General Assembly has more members?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, the House of Representatives has 100 members while the Senate has 50 members. This 2:1 ratio is common in state legislatures.

questions.text: What type of legislature does Indiana have?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, Indiana operates a part-time citizen legislature where members meet for limited sessions each year and maintain other careers. This model differs from full-time professional legislatures in states like California or New York.

questions.text: How many consecutive General Assembly sessions must approve a constitutional amendment before it goes to voters?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, a proposed constitutional amendment must be approved by two consecutive General Assembly sessions before being placed on the ballot for voter approval. This ensures careful deliberation of constitutional changes.

questions.text: What happens after the Indiana General Assembly approves a constitutional amendment in two consecutive sessions?
options: Voters must approve it in an election
The Supreme Court must review it
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, after the General Assembly approves a constitutional amendment in two consecutive sessions, it must be placed on the ballot for voter approval. This gives citizens the final say on changes to the state constitution.

questions.text: Can Indiana citizens propose constitutional amendments through an initiative process?
options: No, only the legislature can propose amendments
Yes, but only for budget matters
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, Indiana does not have a citizen initiative process for constitutional amendments. Only the General Assembly can propose amendments, which must then pass two consecutive sessions and be approved by voters.

questions.text: Who is the current Governor of Indiana as of 2025?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Mike Braun is the current Governor of Indiana. He describes Indiana as 'a beacon of freedom and opportunity, where hard work and Hoosier values build strong families and thriving communities.'

questions.text: What is the term length for the Governor of Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the Governor of Indiana serves a 4-year term. Governors can serve up to two consecutive terms, for a maximum of 8 years total.

questions.text: How many consecutive terms can an Indiana Governor serve?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, a Governor can serve up to two consecutive 4-year terms, for a maximum of 8 years total. After sitting out for at least one term, a former governor could run again.

questions.text: Who is the current Lieutenant Governor of Indiana as of 2025?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Micah Beckwith is the current Lieutenant Governor of Indiana. The Lieutenant Governor is elected on the same ticket as the Governor.

questions.text: Who is the current Secretary of State of Indiana as of 2025?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Diego Morales is the current Secretary of State of Indiana. The Secretary of State is one of several statewide elected executive officers in Indiana.

questions.text: Who is the current Attorney General of Indiana as of 2025?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Todd Rokita is the current Attorney General of Indiana. The Attorney General is elected statewide and serves as the state's chief legal officer.

questions.text: Who is the current Treasurer of State of Indiana as of 2025?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Daniel Elliott is the current Treasurer of State of Indiana. The Treasurer is elected statewide and manages the state's finances and investments.

questions.text: Who is the current State Comptroller of Indiana as of 2025?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Elise Nieshalla is the current State Comptroller of Indiana. The Comptroller is one of the statewide elected executive officers responsible for state financial oversight.

questions.text: How many statewide elected executive officers does Indiana have?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Indiana has six statewide elected executive officers: Governor, Lieutenant Governor, Attorney General, Secretary of State, Treasurer of State, and State Comptroller. This structure distributes executive power across multiple independently elected officials.

questions.text: How are the Governor and Lieutenant Governor of Indiana elected?
options: The Governor appoints the Lieutenant Governor
The legislature elects the Lieutenant Governor
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the Governor and Lieutenant Governor run on the same ticket and are elected together. This ensures they work as a team and share the same electoral mandate.

questions.text: What is Indiana's official state motto?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, Indiana's state motto is 'The Crossroads of America,' reflecting the state's central location and its role as a transportation hub. This motto appears on Indiana license plates and official state materials.

questions.text: Which executive officer manages Indiana's state finances and investments?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, the Treasurer of State manages Indiana's finances and investments. The State Comptroller handles financial oversight and accounting, while the Treasurer focuses on investment management and cash flow.

questions.text: How many levels are in the Indiana state court system?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Judicial Branch, Indiana has a four-level court system: trial courts (circuit and superior courts), the Court of Appeals, the Tax Court, and the Supreme Court at the top.

questions.text: How are Indiana Supreme Court justices selected?
options: Appointment by the Governor from a nominating commission list
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, Supreme Court justices are appointed by the Governor from a list of nominees provided by the Judicial Nominating Commission, then face retention votes.

questions.text: What is the term length for an Indiana Supreme Court justice?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, Supreme Court justices serve 10-year terms and must face retention elections to continue serving additional terms.

questions.text: How many justices serve on the Indiana Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the Indiana Supreme Court consists of five justices, including one Chief Justice who serves as the administrative head of the court system.

questions.text: What type of cases does the Indiana Tax Court handle?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Judicial Branch, the Tax Court is a specialized court that hears appeals and original actions related to state tax matters, including property tax assessments and state tax disputes.

questions.text: What is the mandatory retirement age for Indiana judges?
options: There is no mandatory retirement age
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, judges must retire at age 75, though they may continue to serve as senior judges on a part-time basis after retirement.

questions.text: How many judges serve on the Indiana Court of Appeals?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Judicial Branch, the Court of Appeals consists of 15 judges who hear appeals from trial courts across the state, sitting in panels of three judges.

questions.text: What are the trial courts of general jurisdiction called in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Judicial Branch, Circuit Courts and Superior Courts serve as the trial courts of general jurisdiction, handling civil, criminal, family, and juvenile cases at the county level.

questions.text: How are trial court judges selected in Indiana?
options: Elected by voters in partisan elections
Elected by voters in nonpartisan elections
questions.explanation: According to Indiana law, trial court judges (circuit and superior court judges) are elected by voters in partisan elections, meaning candidates run with party labels on the ballot.

questions.text: What body oversees judicial discipline and conduct in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the Commission on Judicial Qualifications investigates complaints against judges and can recommend discipline, including removal from office, to the Supreme Court.

questions.text: What is the term length for Indiana Court of Appeals judges?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, Court of Appeals judges serve 10-year terms and are appointed by the Governor from a nominating commission list, then face retention elections.

questions.text: Which court has the final authority to interpret the Indiana Constitution?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the Indiana Supreme Court is the highest court in the state and has final authority to interpret the state constitution on matters of state law.

questions.text: What happens when an Indiana appellate judge faces a retention election?
options: Voters choose between the judge and an opponent
Voters decide yes or no on whether the judge should continue serving
The General Assembly votes on retention
The Governor decides whether to reappoint
questions.explanation: According to Indiana law, appellate judges face retention elections where voters simply vote yes or no on whether the judge should remain in office, rather than running against an opponent.

questions.text: In what month are general elections held in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to Indiana election law, general elections are held on the first Tuesday after the first Monday in November in even-numbered years, consistent with federal election timing.

questions.text: How many days before an election must Indiana voters register to vote?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, voters must register at least 29 days before an election to be eligible to vote in that election, whether registering online, by mail, or in person.

questions.text: What state official oversees elections and voter registration in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Secretary of State serves as the chief election officer for Indiana, overseeing voter registration, election administration, and campaign finance reporting.

questions.text: What is required to vote by absentee ballot in Indiana?
options: Any registered voter can vote absentee without a reason
Voters must provide one of several qualifying reasons
Only military and overseas voters can vote absentee
Voters must be over 65 years old
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, voters must have a qualifying reason to vote absentee, such as being absent from the county on election day, having a disability, being over 65, or working as an election officer.

questions.text: At what age can Indiana residents register to vote?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, residents must be 18 years old by the date of the next election to register to vote, consistent with the 26th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution.

questions.text: What form of identification is required to vote in person in Indiana?
options: Any document showing name and address
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, voters must present a government-issued photo ID that shows their name, photo, and expiration date to vote in person at the polls.

questions.text: Who administers elections at the county level in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to Indiana election law, each county has a County Election Board composed of three members who oversee election administration, polling places, and vote counting in their county.

questions.text: When do polls open on election day in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, polling places open at 6:00 AM and close at 6:00 PM local time on election day, providing 12 hours for in-person voting.

questions.text: How can Indiana voters check their voter registration status?
options: Only by visiting the county clerk's office in person
Online through the Secretary of State's website
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, voters can check their registration status online at indianavoters.in.gov, which provides information about registration status, polling location, and sample ballots.

questions.text: What happens if an Indiana voter moves to a new address within the same county?
options: They must re-register to vote entirely
They must update their voter registration with the new address
They can vote at either their old or new precinct
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, voters who move within the same county must update their voter registration with their new address to ensure they vote in the correct precinct.

questions.text: What is the deadline for requesting an absentee ballot in Indiana?
options: The day before the election by noon
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Secretary of State, voters must request an absentee ballot by the day before the election at noon, though it's recommended to request earlier to ensure time for mailing and return.

questions.text: How many consecutive sessions must the Indiana General Assembly approve a constitutional amendment before it goes to voters?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, a proposed amendment must be approved by the General Assembly in two consecutive sessions before being placed on the ballot for voter approval. This ensures careful deliberation of constitutional changes.

questions.text: Can Indiana citizens place initiatives directly on the ballot through petition signatures?
options: No, only the legislature can propose amendments
Yes, but only for local ordinances
questions.explanation: According to Indiana law, the state does not have a citizen initiative process. Constitutional amendments and statutory changes must be proposed by the General Assembly. This makes Indiana different from states like California that allow direct citizen initiatives.

questions.text: What year did Indiana become the 19th state admitted to the Union?
questions.explanation: According to historical records, Indiana was admitted to the Union on December 11, 1816, becoming the 19th state. This followed the Northwest Ordinance process and came just five years after the Battle of Tippecanoe.

questions.text: After the General Assembly approves a constitutional amendment twice, who makes the final decision?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, after the General Assembly approves an amendment in two consecutive sessions, it must be submitted to the voters in a general election. A majority vote is required for the amendment to be ratified, ensuring citizens have the final say on constitutional changes.

questions.text: Which body has the sole authority to propose amendments to the Indiana Constitution?
options: A citizen petition with sufficient signatures
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, only the General Assembly has the power to propose amendments to the state constitution. Unlike some states with initiative processes, Indiana citizens cannot directly propose constitutional amendments through petitions.

questions.text: In what month did Indiana achieve statehood?
questions.explanation: According to historical records, Indiana was admitted to the Union on December 11, 1816. This December admission date is commemorated annually as Indiana Day, celebrating the state's entry into the Union.

questions.text: What percentage of voter approval is required to ratify a constitutional amendment in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, a proposed constitutional amendment requires approval by a simple majority of voters casting ballots on that question. This is a lower threshold than the two-thirds legislative approval needed in consecutive sessions.

questions.text: Between which two General Assembly sessions must a constitutional amendment be approved?
options: Two sessions in the same year
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, a proposed amendment must pass in two consecutive sessions of the General Assembly. This means if an amendment passes in the 2025 session, it must also pass in the 2026 session before going to voters.

questions.text: What document established the process for Indiana's path to statehood?
questions.explanation: According to historical records, the Northwest Ordinance of 1787 established the process for territories in the Northwest Territory, including Indiana, to achieve statehood. This federal law set requirements for population, government structure, and constitutional conventions.

questions.text: If a constitutional amendment passes the General Assembly in 2025, when is the earliest it could appear on the ballot?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, an amendment must pass two consecutive General Assembly sessions before going to voters. If it passes in 2025, it must also pass in the 2026 session, then appear on the ballot in the 2026 general election.

questions.text: What type of election must constitutional amendments appear on in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, proposed constitutional amendments must be submitted to voters at a general election, not primary or special elections. This ensures maximum voter participation in decisions about constitutional changes.

questions.text: How many times has the Indiana Constitution been replaced since statehood?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Historical Bureau, Indiana adopted its first constitution in 1816 at statehood, then replaced it with a second constitution in 1851, which remains in effect today with amendments. The 1851 constitution addressed issues like debt limits and legislative structure.

questions.text: Can the Governor of Indiana veto a constitutional amendment passed by the General Assembly?
options: Yes, with a two-thirds override possible
Yes, but only once per term
No, the Governor has no role in the amendment process
No, but the Governor can delay it for one session
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the Governor plays no role in the constitutional amendment process. Amendments require approval by the General Assembly in two consecutive sessions, then go directly to voters without gubernatorial involvement.

questions.text: What happens if a constitutional amendment fails to pass in the second consecutive General Assembly session?
options: It automatically goes to voters anyway
The process must start over from the beginning
The Governor can force a vote
It goes to the Indiana Supreme Court
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, if a proposed amendment fails to pass in the second consecutive session, the entire process must restart. The amendment would need to pass two consecutive sessions again before going to voters.

questions.text: What was Indiana's status before becoming a state in 1816?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Historical Bureau, Indiana was part of the Northwest Territory before achieving statehood. The territory was organized under the Northwest Ordinance of 1787 and included land that would become several Midwestern states.

questions.text: What vote threshold is required in each General Assembly session for a constitutional amendment to advance?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, a proposed constitutional amendment requires a simple majority vote in both the House and Senate in each of the two consecutive sessions. This is less stringent than the federal constitutional amendment process.

questions.text: Which elected statewide office in Indiana is responsible for managing state finances and investments?
questions.explanation: According to IN.gov, the Treasurer of State is responsible for managing state funds and investments. Indiana elects multiple statewide officers, including the Treasurer, who serves independently of the Governor.

questions.text: Why does Indiana require constitutional amendments to pass two consecutive General Assembly sessions?
options: To allow the Governor time to review
To ensure careful deliberation of constitutional changes
To allow the Supreme Court to review
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the two-session requirement ensures careful deliberation of constitutional changes. This process allows time for public input, legislative debate, and reconsideration before amendments go to voters, protecting the constitution from hasty changes.

questions.text: How many senators serve in the Indiana State Senate?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, the Indiana State Senate consists of 50 senators who serve four-year terms, representing districts across the state.

questions.text: How many representatives serve in the Indiana House of Representatives?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana General Assembly, the Indiana House of Representatives has 100 members who serve two-year terms, making it twice the size of the state Senate.

questions.text: How many days does the Indiana General Assembly meet during odd-numbered years?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the General Assembly meets for 61 days in odd-numbered years to conduct legislative business, including passing the state budget. This part-time legislature model allows legislators to maintain other careers.

questions.text: How many days does the Indiana General Assembly meet during even-numbered years?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution, the General Assembly meets for 30 days in even-numbered years, which is a shorter session than odd-numbered years when the budget is passed.

questions.text: What is the relationship between the Indiana Constitution and the U.S. Constitution?
options: The Indiana Constitution must comply with the U.S. Constitution but can provide additional rights
The Indiana Constitution completely replaces the U.S. Constitution within state borders
The U.S. Constitution only applies to federal matters and has no effect on state law
The Indiana Constitution and U.S. Constitution are completely independent documents
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Constitution and principles of federalism, state constitutions must comply with the U.S. Constitution as the supreme law of the land, but states can provide additional rights and protections to their citizens beyond what the federal constitution guarantees. This is why Indiana has its own bill of rights that mirrors and sometimes expands upon federal protections.

questions.text: What number state was Indiana when it joined the Union?
questions.explanation: According to the Indiana Historical Bureau, Indiana became the 19th state admitted to the United States when it joined the Union on December 11, 1816.

questions.text: Which state agency handles driver's license renewals in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Bureau of Motor Vehicles (BMV) is the state agency responsible for driver's license services, vehicle registration, and other motor vehicle-related functions in Indiana.

questions.text: Which Indiana state agency manages fish and wildlife resources?
options: Department of Natural Resources - Fish and Wildlife Division
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Fish and Wildlife division manages Indiana's fish and wildlife resources, including hunting and fishing licenses, conservation programs, and wildlife habitat protection.

questions.text: What is the role of Indiana's State Comptroller?
options: Oversees state accounting and financial reporting
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the State Comptroller, currently Elise Nieshalla, oversees state accounting, financial reporting, and ensures proper management of state financial records and transactions.

questions.text: What is the role of Indiana's Treasurer of State?
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Treasurer of State, currently Daniel Elliott, is responsible for managing and investing state funds, ensuring the state's financial resources are properly safeguarded and earn appropriate returns.

questions.text: Which state agency oversees Indiana's roads and highways?
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Indiana Department of Transportation (INDOT) is responsible for planning, building, and maintaining the state's roads, highways, and bridges, ensuring safe and efficient transportation infrastructure.

questions.text: Which state agency manages Indiana's state parks and camping facilities?
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Department of Natural Resources manages Indiana's state parks, camping facilities, and outdoor recreation areas, preserving natural resources while providing public access to outdoor activities.

questions.text: Which state agency issues professional licenses in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Indiana Professional Licensing Agency oversees the licensing and regulation of various professions in the state, ensuring professionals meet required standards to protect public safety.

questions.text: How can Indiana residents contact their state legislators?
options: Through the General Assembly website or local district offices
Only by visiting the State House in person
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, Indiana residents can contact their state representatives and senators through the General Assembly website, by phone, email, or by visiting district offices. This accessibility ensures citizens can engage with their elected officials year-round.

questions.text: What online service does IN.gov provide for state tax filing?
options: Online state tax filing through the Department of Revenue
Tax filing only through private companies
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, Indiana residents can file their state taxes online through the Department of Revenue's website, making tax compliance more convenient and accessible for citizens.

questions.text: Which state agency oversees public health services in Indiana?
questions.explanation: According to in.gov, the Indiana Department of Health is responsible for public health services, disease prevention, health promotion, and protecting the health and safety of all Hoosiers.

questions.text: What type of government structure does Indiana use at the local level?
questions.explanation: According to Indiana government sources, Indiana has a strong township government structure that is unique among states. Townships provide local services and governance at the most local level, working alongside county and municipal governments.

source.name: Indiana Association of Cities and Towns

questions.text: Indiana's 1816 constitution was notably progressive for its time — what did it uniquely require the state to fund?
options: A free public education system open to all
A state militia funded by property taxes
A network of toll-free roads connecting county seats
A public hospital in every county seat
questions.explanation: Indiana's first constitution (1816) included a provision requiring the state to establish a general system of education, ascending in a regular graduation from township schools to a state university, tuition-free and open to all. This was a rare civic commitment for a newly admitted state at the time.

source.name: Indiana State Library – Indiana Constitution of 1816

questions.text: How many votes of the Indiana General Assembly are required to propose an amendment to the Indiana Constitution?
options: A majority vote in two consecutive sessions of the General Assembly
A two-thirds vote in a single session of the General Assembly
A three-fourths vote in a single session of the General Assembly
A majority vote in a single session followed by a statewide convention
questions.explanation: Indiana's amendment process requires a proposed amendment to pass a majority vote in two consecutive sessions of the General Assembly, after which voters ratify it in a general election. This dual-session requirement makes Indiana's constitution harder to amend than many states. See Indiana Constitution, Article 16.

questions.text: Indiana held a constitutional convention in 1850–51. What major change did the new constitution make to how judges were selected?
options: Judges became popularly elected rather than appointed by the legislature
The governor gained sole authority to appoint all judges
A permanent Supreme Court was established for the first time
Judges were required to be approved by a statewide referendum every two years
questions.explanation: Indiana's 1851 Constitution shifted judicial selection from legislative appointment to popular election, reflecting the Jacksonian democratic reform movement sweeping states at the time. This convention also addressed debt limits and banking after fiscal crises under the 1816 constitution. See: Indiana Historical Bureau.

source.name: Indiana Historical Bureau – Indiana Constitution of 1851

questions.text: How many votes are required in each chamber of the Indiana General Assembly to override a governor's veto?
questions.explanation: Article 5, Section 14 of the Indiana Constitution allows the General Assembly to override a governor's veto with a majority of all members elected to each chamber — not a two-thirds supermajority as many states require. This makes Indiana's veto override threshold relatively accessible compared to federal and many state standards.

questions.text: Indiana's 1851 constitution made which major change from its original 1816 constitution?
options: It banned the state government from going into debt for internal improvements
It abolished the office of lieutenant governor entirely
It granted voting rights to women in local elections
It moved the state capital from Corydon to Indianapolis
questions.explanation: After costly canal and railroad debts nearly bankrupted Indiana, the 1851 constitution prohibited the state from contracting debt for internal improvements — a direct lesson from the Mammoth Internal Improvement Act of 1836 disaster. The capital had already moved to Indianapolis in 1825, well before 1851.

source.name: Indiana State Library – Indiana Constitution History

questions.text: Indiana's constitution can be amended only if approved by voters — but how many consecutive General Assemblies must first pass the proposed amendment?
options: One General Assembly with a supermajority
One General Assembly followed by a governor's signature
questions.explanation: Under Article 16 of the Indiana Constitution, a proposed amendment must pass two successive General Assemblies (with an intervening election after the first passage) before it goes to voters for ratification. This two-session requirement gives citizens a chance to weigh in through elections before a final vote. Source: Indiana Constitution, Article 16.

source.name: Indiana General Assembly – Indiana Constitution Article 16

questions.text: Can Indiana voters cast their ballot before Election Day?
options: No, only absentee mail voting is allowed
No, Indiana requires all voting on Election Day
Yes, but only for voters over age 65
questions.explanation: Indiana offers early in-person voting (called 'absentee voting in person') at county clerk offices and satellite locations starting 28 days before an election. Any registered voter may participate, with no excuse required.

questions.text: Indiana's 1816 constitution was notable for which progressive civic provision?
options: It guaranteed free public education for all white children
It abolished slavery entirely within the state
It granted voting rights to free Black men
questions.explanation: Indiana's 1816 constitution prohibited slavery, making it one of the first states to explicitly ban the practice upon entering the Union. This was a significant civic milestone, though full equality remained elusive for decades after. Source: Indiana State Library.

source.name: Indiana State Archives – Indiana Constitution of 1816

questions.text: How many articles does the Indiana Constitution contain?
questions.explanation: The Indiana Constitution, adopted in 1851, is organized into 16 articles covering topics from the Bill of Rights to local government. It remains one of the foundational documents of Indiana governance.

questions.text: How does Indiana fund its public schools — what is the primary mechanism?
options: A per-pupil foundation amount set by the state legislature and distributed through the school funding formula
Local property taxes collected by each school district and kept entirely within that district
A flat block grant divided equally among all school districts regardless of enrollment
Federal Title I funds distributed by the Indiana Department of Education based on poverty rates
questions.explanation: Indiana uses a state foundation funding formula that calculates a per-pupil amount, then distributes dollars to districts based on enrollment. This system was designed to reduce reliance on local property taxes and promote more equal funding statewide. See Indiana's biennial budget and education funding at the Indiana General Assembly website.

questions.text: How many votes in the Indiana General Assembly are required to propose an amendment to the Indiana Constitution?
options: A simple majority in both chambers in two consecutive sessions
Two-thirds of both chambers in a single session
Three-fourths of both chambers in two consecutive sessions
A simple majority in one session, then ratified by a statewide convention
questions.explanation: Under Article 16 of the Indiana Constitution, an amendment must pass both chambers by a simple majority in two successive General Assemblies, then be approved by voters at a general election. This two-session requirement helps prevent hasty changes to the state's foundational law.

source.name: Indiana Constitution – Article 16, Indiana General Assembly

questions.text: How many votes does the Indiana General Assembly need to override a governor's veto?
options: A simple majority in each chamber
A two-thirds majority in each chamber
A three-fourths majority in the Senate only
questions.explanation: Indiana's Constitution (Article 5, Section 14) allows the General Assembly to override a governor's veto with a majority of all elected members in each chamber — notably easier than the two-thirds supermajority required in many other states and by the U.S. Constitution.

questions.text: What is the title of Indiana's second-highest executive officer?
questions.explanation: Indiana's Lieutenant Governor is first in line to succeed the Governor and also serves as a key member of the executive branch. As of 2026-02-24, the Lt. Governor chairs several state agencies and boards.

questions.text: Indiana requires photo ID to vote. Which document is NOT accepted as valid voter ID at Indiana polls?
options: A utility bill with your name and address
An Indiana state-issued photo ID card
questions.explanation: Indiana's photo ID law, upheld by the Supreme Court in Crawford v. Marion County (2008), requires a government-issued photo ID. A utility bill, even with your address, does not qualify because it lacks a photo. Accepted IDs include driver's licenses, state photo IDs, military IDs, and U.S. passports.

questions.text: How can Indiana citizens propose a change to the state constitution?
options: Only through the state legislature passing a measure twice across two sessions
By collecting signatures from 5% of voters and submitting a petition
The governor can propose amendments directly to voters
A single majority vote in the General Assembly is sufficient
questions.explanation: Indiana does not allow citizen-initiated constitutional amendments. Changes must pass both chambers of the General Assembly in two consecutive legislative sessions, then be approved by voters at a general election. This two-session requirement gives Hoosiers time to debate proposed changes.

source.name: Indiana General Assembly – Indiana Constitution Article 16

questions.text: Indiana's 1816 constitution was drafted at a convention in which town, chosen as a temporary meeting place before a permanent capital was established?
questions.explanation: Delegates met in Corydon in June 1816 to draft Indiana's first state constitution. Corydon served as Indiana's first state capital until 1825, when the capital moved to the newly founded Indianapolis. Indiana Statehouse records document this founding milestone.

source.name: Indiana State Library - Indiana Constitution History

questions.text: Which branch of government does the Indiana Constitution establish first?
questions.explanation: Like the U.S. Constitution, Indiana's constitution places the legislative branch first, reflecting the founders' view that lawmaking power is closest to the people. Article 4 of the Indiana Constitution establishes the General Assembly.

questions.text: How does Indiana fund its public K-12 schools under the state's tuition support formula?
options: Per-pupil funding follows the student to whichever accredited school they attend, including charter and private voucher schools
Each school district receives a flat block grant based on its geographic size, not enrollment
Local property taxes are the sole source of K-12 funding, with no state contribution
The state funds only teachers' salaries, while districts must raise all other costs locally
questions.explanation: Indiana uses a student-centered funding model: state tuition support dollars are allocated on a per-pupil basis and can follow students to charter schools or private schools through the Choice Scholarship (voucher) program. This makes Indiana's school finance system one of the most expansive choice-based models in the U.S. See Indiana Department of Education school funding overview.

source.name: Indiana Department of Education – School Funding

questions.text: What are the two chambers of Indiana's General Assembly?
questions.explanation: Like most U.S. state legislatures, Indiana's General Assembly has two chambers: a Senate (50 members) and a House of Representatives (100 members). Together they write, debate, and pass state laws.

questions.text: Who serves as the Governor of Indiana as of 2026-02-24?
questions.explanation: Mike Braun was inaugurated as Indiana's 52nd Governor in January 2025 after winning the 2024 gubernatorial election. He previously served as a U.S. Senator from Indiana.

questions.text: What is the highest court in Indiana's state court system?
questions.explanation: The Indiana Supreme Court is the highest court in the state, with five justices who have final say on Indiana law. It reviews decisions from lower courts and oversees the state's entire judicial branch.

questions.text: Indiana requires voters to show photo ID at the polls. Which document is NOT accepted as valid voter ID under Indiana law?
options: A utility bill with your name and address
An Indiana state-issued photo ID card
questions.explanation: Indiana's photo ID law, upheld by the U.S. Supreme Court in Crawford v. Marion County (2008), requires government-issued photo ID. Utility bills, bank statements, and similar documents lack a photo and are not accepted at Indiana polling places.

source.name: Indiana Election Division - Voter ID Requirements

questions.text: How can Indiana citizens propose a change to the state constitution?
options: Only the state legislature can propose constitutional amendments
Citizens can gather signatures to put amendments directly on the ballot
The governor submits proposed amendments to voters
A state court must first approve any proposed amendment
questions.explanation: Unlike some states, Indiana does not have a citizen initiative process for constitutional amendments. Only the Indiana General Assembly can propose amendments, which must pass two consecutive sessions before going to voters for ratification.

source.name: Indiana General Assembly – Constitutional Amendment Process

questions.text: How many votes in the Indiana General Assembly are required to override a governor's veto?
options: A simple majority in each chamber
A two-thirds majority in each chamber
A three-fifths majority in each chamber
A unanimous vote in the Senate only
questions.explanation: Indiana's Constitution (Article 5, Section 14) requires a two-thirds majority in both the House and Senate to override a governor's veto, making it harder for the legislature to override executive vetoes than a simple majority would allow.

questions.text: What type of tax is Indiana's main source of state revenue?
questions.explanation: Indiana relies primarily on the sales tax as its largest source of state revenue. The state sales tax rate is 7%, one of the highest flat rates in the nation, and it funds key services like education and infrastructure. Indiana Fiscal Policy Institute and the Indiana Department of Revenue confirm sales tax as the top revenue driver.

questions.text: Which Indiana executive officer is constitutionally required to serve as President of the State Senate?
questions.explanation: Indiana's Lieutenant Governor is constitutionally designated as President of the State Senate, a unique dual role blending executive and legislative duties. This makes the office more powerful than in many other states, where the lieutenant governor has no formal legislative role.

source.name: Indiana Constitution, Article 5 – Indiana General Assembly

questions.text: Does Indiana require voters to show photo ID at the polls?
options: Yes, Indiana was the first state to enact a strict photo ID law
No, any signed document with your name is acceptable
Only first-time voters must show photo ID
Photo ID is requested but not required
questions.explanation: Indiana passed the nation's first strict photo ID voting law in 2005, upheld by the U.S. Supreme Court in Crawford v. Marion County Election Board (2008). Voters must show a government-issued photo ID to cast a regular ballot.

questions.text: What was significant about Indiana becoming the 19th U.S. state in 1816?
options: It gave Indiana citizens the right to elect their own state lawmakers and governor
It allowed Indiana to collect federal taxes independently
It made Indiana the first state carved from the Northwest Territory
It immediately granted Indiana two seats in the House of Representatives
questions.explanation: When Indiana achieved statehood in 1816, residents gained full self-governance — including the ability to elect their own legislature and governor — rather than living under a federally appointed territorial government. This shift from territory to state is a core civic milestone. Ohio was actually the first Northwest Territory state (1803).

questions.text: How many votes in the Indiana General Assembly are required to propose a constitutional amendment?
options: A simple majority in both chambers in two consecutive sessions
Two-thirds of both chambers in a single session
Three-fourths of both chambers in two consecutive sessions
A simple majority in one session, then two-thirds in the next
questions.explanation: To amend Indiana's constitution, a majority of both the House and Senate must approve the amendment in two separate General Assemblies (with an election in between), then Indiana voters ratify it. This double-session requirement gives citizens a chance to weigh in by electing new legislators before the final vote. See Indiana Constitution, Article 16.

questions.text: How does Indiana fund its public K-12 schools — what is the primary mechanism?
options: A per-pupil 'foundation' grant distributed by the state based on enrollment
Local property taxes collected and kept entirely within each school district
A flat block grant divided equally among all school districts regardless of size
Federal Title I funds allocated directly to districts by the U.S. Department of Education
questions.explanation: Indiana uses a foundation funding formula that distributes state dollars on a per-pupil basis, meaning schools receive more total funding as enrollment grows. This model was designed to reduce reliance on local property taxes and narrow funding gaps between wealthy and poor districts. Indiana Department of Education, https://www.in.gov/doe/

source.name: Indiana Department of Education – School Funding
